lesson.menu_desc: Hotel requests & complaints, reported questions & a reading on great stays -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- The Perfect Stay: Hotels
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 7 -- The Perfect Stay: Hotels

reading.rtitle: The Night the Concierge Saved the Trip
paras: When the Vasquez family finally reached the Marlowe Hotel, well after midnight, they were close to tears. Their connecting flight had been cancelled, their luggage was somewhere over the Atlantic, and the youngest child had a fever. The night concierge, a softly spoken man named Theo, listened without interrupting. He did not promise miracles. Instead, he quietly asked whether anyone in the family needed medicine, and he told the parents to leave everything to him for the next hour.

By the time the family had washed and changed into the spare clothes housekeeping had found, Theo had already escalated two requests. He had asked the on-call doctor to come up to the suite, and he had asked the airline to flag the missing bags as urgent. There was a small discrepancy on the reservation, but rather than argue about it at one in the morning, he simply absorbed the cost as a courtesy and moved on. The parents later said it was the calm, not the perks, that they remembered.

A few weeks afterwards, the family wrote a letter to the hotel manager. They did not mention the upgrade, the late check-out, or the complimentary breakfast. They wrote about a stranger who had asked the right questions, told them not to worry, and made an impossible night feel manageable. A great hotel, the letter concluded, is not measured by its marble lobby but by the people who decide, at the worst possible moment, to be kind.
reading.source: Adapted from a hospitality column, The Long Corridor

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about the Marlowe Hotel?
choices: The family was impressed mainly by the free upgrade and the luxurious lobby.
What made the stay memorable was a staff member's calm, kindness and quick action, not the perks.
The hotel handled the missing luggage badly and lost the family's trust.

items: The Vasquez family arrived at the hotel early in the evening.
f
They reached the Marlowe well after midnight, close to tears.
Theo escalated requests, contacting both a doctor and the airline.
t
He asked the on-call doctor to come up and flagged the bags as urgent with the airline.
Theo argued about the discrepancy on the reservation at one in the morning.

Rather than argue, he absorbed the cost as a courtesy and moved on.
In their letter, the family praised the marble lobby and the upgrade above all.
f
They wrote about the kindness of a stranger, not the perks or the lobby.
Scenario 1
At breakfast, a friend asks what the receptionist wanted to know when you checked in. Report her question out loud, beginning: "She asked whether..."
Scenario 2
You phoned housekeeping last night. Tell your travel companion what you asked them to do, beginning: "I asked housekeeping to..."
Scenario 3
The concierge gave you a firm safety instruction about the room safe. Report it, beginning: "He told me not to..."
Scenario 4
There was a problem with your bill. Explain to a colleague what you asked the manager to do, beginning: "I asked the manager to..."

analyse.title: Look closely at the text
items: Find the two reported questions Theo asks in the first paragraph and rewrite each as the original direct question.
The writer says "it was the calm, not the perks, that they remembered." Explain in your own words what this contrast tells us about good service.
The final sentence contrasts a "marble lobby" with "the people who decide to be kind." Why do you think the writer ends on people rather than on facilities?

listenings.text: Good evening, this is a message from the front desk for the guest in room four-one-two. Earlier you asked whether late check-out was possible, and I am pleased to confirm that we can offer it until two o'clock at no extra charge. You also asked the concierge to arrange a taxi to the airport; that is now booked for half past two. Finally, regarding the noise you reported last night, the manager has asked me to apologise and has upgraded your room to a quiet suite on the top floor for your final evening. Please do let us know if there is anything else we can do.

Honestly, that hotel turned a tricky night into a lovely stay. When I checked in, the receptionist asked whether I had stayed with them before, and she told me to keep the welcome drink voucher for the rooftop bar. There was a small discrepancy on my bill, so I asked the manager to look into it, and within minutes he had removed the charge and added a courtesy breakfast. I learned something on this trip: if you stay calm and explain the problem clearly, most hotels will go out of their way to accommodate you. A good hotel is not the one where nothing goes wrong, but the one that puts it right.

extra_audio.text: First, she asked whether the hotel had any vacancies for the night. Then she requested a late check-out and an upgrade to a suite. The concierge told her to relax and arranged a table for dinner. Later, she noticed a discrepancy on the bill and decided to escalate the complaint. Finally, as a courtesy, the manager removed the charge and apologised.
